mechanism_narrative: A federal funds rate that has climbed steadily above 4% and continues rising raises the discount rate applied to equity cash flows and tightens financial conditions, producing negative S&P 500 forward returns over the next 20 trading days as valuation multiples compress.
evidence_refs: fact:FEDFUNDS at 4.29 percent up from sub-1 percent levels in 2004
failure_condition: Rank IC between FEDFUNDS level trend and forward 20d returns stays below 0.03 over any rolling 2-year window, or returns following rate increases are not significantly more negative than the unconditional mean (p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate falls and holds below 4.7 percent for three or more consecutive months, labor markets are historically tight enough to trigger accelerated Fed tightening expectations, which depresses S&P 500 forward returns over the following quarter.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE stays below 4.7% for 3 consecutive months
evidence_refs: fact:UNRATE at 4.7 percent, near cycle lows in early 2006
regime_hint: late-cycle expansion with tight labor market
failure_condition: In periods where UNRATE sustains below 4.7% for 3+ months, 60-day forward SPX returns are positive in more than 55% of instances, or Rank IC < 0.03 over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Joint deterioration of housing starts alongside a rising federal funds rate signals an interest-rate-driven housing slowdown that propagates into broader consumer demand and corporate earnings, producing negative S&P 500 returns over the next quarter; neither series alone captures this transmission because housing weakness without monetary tightening is often idiosyncratic supply adjustment.
candidate_trigger: HOUST declines more than 10% from 12-month peak while FEDFUNDS exceeds 4%
evidence_refs: fact:HOUST at 1933k units, down from 2005 peaks near 2100k while FEDFUNDS at 4.29%
regime_hint: monetary tightening cycle with housing cooldown
failure_condition: When both trigger conditions are met, 60-day forward returns are not significantly more negative than the unconditional distribution (p > 0.05), or the joint signal underperforms either single-series baseline in Rank IC.

mechanism_narrative: Industrial production growth predicts positive S&P 500 forward returns during normal expansions by signaling healthy corporate output, but this relationship inverts in a late-cycle regime where the federal funds rate is elevated above 4%, because rising output then reinforces inflation and tightening fears rather than earnings optimism.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS > 4.0% and INDPRO month-over-month growth positive
evidence_refs: fact:INDPRO at 110.269 with FEDFUNDS at 4.29 percent indicating late-cycle overlap
failure_condition: Positive INDPRO growth continues to predict positive 20-day forward returns even when FEDFUNDS > 4%, i.e., the sign does not flip relative to the low-rate regime baseline; reject if Rank IC sign is unchanged across regimes.

mechanism_narrative: Rising retail sales reflect resilient consumer spending that supports corporate revenue growth, producing modest positive S&P 500 forward returns over the next week as investors extrapolate near-term earnings strength.
evidence_refs: fact:RSAFS at 365,369 million dollars showing continued nominal sales growth
failure_condition: Rank IC between month-over-month RSAFS growth and forward 5-day returns is below 0.02 over any rolling 2-year window, or the sign of the average return response is negative in more than 55% of positive-growth months.

mechanism_narrative: Once the federal funds rate crosses above 4.5%, monetary policy transitions from neutral to overtly restrictive, raising real borrowing costs enough to compress equity valuations and produce negative S&P 500 forward returns over the following month.
evidence_refs: fact:FEDFUNDS at 4.29 percent approaching the 4.5 percent threshold in early 2006
failure_condition: Forward 20-day returns following the first crossing above 4.5% are not significantly negative relative to baseline (p > 0.05), or Rank IC < 0.03 across the tightening episode.

mechanism_narrative: A joint pattern of decelerating nonfarm payroll growth combined with a rising unemployment rate signals an inflection from labor market strength to weakness, and this combination predicts negative S&P 500 forward returns over the next week because it front-runs recognized recession risk before either series alone crosses a conventional threshold.
candidate_trigger: PAYEMS 3-month average growth decelerates while UNRATE rises for 2 consecutive months
evidence_refs: fact:PAYEMS at 134,564k and UNRATE at 4.7% as of early 2006
failure_condition: Instances satisfying both PAYEMS deceleration and UNRATE increase do not show significantly negative forward 5-day returns versus baseline (p > 0.05), or the joint signal's Rank IC is not higher than either single-series signal.

mechanism_narrative: Industrial production deceleration occurring while CPI inflation remains elevated signals emerging stagflation risk, where weak output growth coincides with persistent price pressure, leading investors to discount equities further and producing negative S&P 500 forward returns over the next quarter.
evidence_refs: fact:CPIAUCSL at 197.7 with INDPRO growth moderating from prior quarters
regime_hint: high inflation regime with slowing output growth
failure_condition: During periods of elevated CPIAUCSL year-over-year growth combined with INDPRO deceleration, 60-day forward returns are not significantly negative relative to baseline (p > 0.05), or Rank IC < 0.03 over any 3-year rolling window.
